title: The Moon
lede: A quarter the width of Earth and a quarter of a million miles away, the Moon is the one other world humans have walked upon. It steadies our planet's spin, raises the tides, lights the night — and it was born, most likely, from a catastrophic collision in the Solar System's violent youth.

maps.title: The near side of the Moon
maps.caption: The dark basaltic "seas" (maria), the bright cratered highlands, and the great ray-craters like Tycho and Copernicus.

images.title: Earthrise
images.caption: Our planet rising over the lunar horizon — the 1968 photograph from Apollo 8 that changed how humanity saw itself.

images.title: Footprint in the dust
images.caption: A bootprint pressed into the lunar soil in 1969 — undisturbed by wind or rain, it may last for millions of years.

images.title: The far side
images.caption: The hemisphere that never faces Earth — more rugged, heavily cratered, and unseen by human eyes until 1959.

videos.title: How the Moon was born
videos.caption: The giant-impact hypothesis — how a Mars-sized world struck the young Earth and flung the Moon into being.

videos.title: Why the Moon drives the tides
videos.caption: The gentle gravitational tug that raises and lowers the world's oceans twice a day.

body.text: A World Born of Catastrophe

The leading explanation for the Moon's origin is also one of the most dramatic stories in planetary science. About 4.5 billion years ago, when the [Solar System](/article/the-solar-system) was young and crowded, a Mars-sized body — sometimes named **Theia** — is thought to have slammed into the newly formed Earth. The colossal impact threw a huge spray of molten and vaporised rock into orbit, and from that ring of debris the Moon swiftly coalesced.

This **giant-impact hypothesis** explains several puzzling facts at once: why the Moon is relatively large compared with its planet, why it has only a small iron core, and why Moon rocks share so close a chemical kinship with Earth's mantle. The Moon, in this view, is partly made of the Earth itself.

body.title: A surprisingly large moon
body.text: The Moon is more than a quarter of Earth's diameter — a far greater ratio than any other major moon in the Solar System. It is so large relative to its planet that some astronomers describe the Earth–Moon system as almost a double world.
The Face We Always See

The Moon is **tidally locked** to the Earth, meaning it rotates exactly once for every orbit it makes. As a result, it always keeps the same hemisphere turned toward us. The familiar "near side" is what every human before the space age ever saw; the **far side** — often wrongly called the "dark side," though it gets just as much sunlight — was a complete mystery until a Soviet spacecraft photographed it in 1959.

The two faces look strikingly different. The near side is marked by broad, dark plains called **maria**, or "seas" — not water, but ancient floods of basalt lava that filled giant impact basins. The far side is more rugged and heavily cratered, with far fewer maria, for reasons still being studied.
A Cratered, Airless Land

With almost no atmosphere, the Moon is a still and silent place. There is no wind, no weather, no sound, and nothing to soften the harsh extremes: surface temperatures swing from around 120°C in the blazing lunar day to below −170°C in the long, frigid night. With no air to burn them up, meteorites strike the surface directly, and over billions of years they have churned it into a fine grey dust called **regolith**.

Because there is no erosion, the Moon is a preserved record of the Solar System's history. The craters tell of an era of heavy bombardment that the Earth has long since erased — and a single human footprint left in 1969 may survive, undisturbed, for millions of years.
Why the Tides Rise

The Moon's gravity pulls on the Earth, and on its oceans most visibly of all. The water on the side facing the Moon is tugged into a bulge, with a matching bulge on the opposite side — and as the Earth rotates beneath these bulges, every coast experiences the rhythmic rise and fall of the **tides**, usually twice a day.

The Moon does more than move the seas. By gravitationally steadying the tilt of Earth's axis, it keeps our climate relatively stable over long ages — a quiet but profound gift to the development of life.

That same gravitational interaction is slowly pushing the Moon away: it recedes from Earth by about 3.8 centimetres a year, and Earth's days grow imperceptibly longer as a result. Long ago the Moon loomed much larger in the sky than it does today.
The Day We Walked There

The Moon is the only world beyond Earth that human beings have visited. On **20 July 1969**, NASA's **Apollo 11** landed and **Neil Armstrong** stepped onto the surface with the words, "That's one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind." Over six successful Apollo landings between 1969 and 1972, **twelve astronauts** walked on the Moon, returning with hundreds of kilograms of rock that transformed our understanding of its history.

The Apollo programme, and the space race that drove it, is told in full in [space missions](/article/space-missions). Those samples confirmed the Moon's great age and lent crucial support to the giant-impact story of its birth.
Going Back to Stay

After half a century away, humanity is returning — and this time the goal is not flags and footprints but a lasting presence. NASA's **Artemis** programme, working with international and commercial partners, aims to land astronauts near the Moon's **south pole**, where deep craters hold water ice in permanent shadow.

That ice is the prize. It could supply drinking water and breathable oxygen, and be split into hydrogen and oxygen for rocket fuel — turning the Moon into a refuelling stop and a stepping stone deeper into space. A base on the Moon would be a proving ground for the technologies needed to reach [Mars](/article/mars) and beyond, a future explored in [the future of space](/article/the-future-of-space). Our oldest companion in the sky may yet become humanity's first home away from Earth.

title: The Nile River
eyebrow: The river that made a nation
lede: The longest river in Africa and the artery of a civilization — the Nile flows north for over six thousand kilometres, and for five thousand years its rhythms dictated where, when, and how Egyptians lived.

maps.title: The Nile basin
maps.caption: From the highlands of East Africa to the Mediterranean delta, draining eleven countries.

images.title: The green valley from above
images.caption: A thin ribbon of cultivation cutting through the desert — visible even from orbit.

images.title: The Nile delta
images.caption: The fan of fertile distributaries where the river meets the Mediterranean.

videos.title: Source to sea
videos.caption: Following the White and Blue Niles from their sources to the delta.

citations.label: Encyclopaedia — the Nile
citations.source: Reference geography of the Nile basin

body.text: Two Rivers Become One

The Nile is really two great rivers joined. The **White Nile** rises in the Great Lakes region of East Africa and flows steadily year-round; the **Blue Nile** descends from the Ethiopian Highlands, swollen each summer by monsoon rains. They meet at **Khartoum** in modern Sudan and run north as a single mighty stream to the Mediterranean. It was the Blue Nile's summer surge that produced Egypt's life-giving **annual flood**.
The Engine of Egyptian Life
Because rain almost never falls on the Egyptian valley, the river was everything:

items: It **watered the fields**, depositing fertile black silt each year.
It was the **highway** of the land — flowing north, with a wind blowing south, so boats could travel both ways.
It set the **calendar**, divided into three seasons: *Akhet* (flood), *Peret* (growing), and *Shemu* (harvest).
It shaped **settlement**, concentrating people on the narrow strip of green between the deserts.

body.title: A river you can see from space
body.text: From orbit, Egypt is a stark image: a brilliant green thread and its delta, surrounded by tan desert. More than 90% of Egyptians still live on the few percent of the land the Nile makes habitable.
The River Today

The [Aswan High Dam](/article/modern-egypt) ended the natural flood in 1970, trading the silt cycle for stored water and electricity. Today the Nile remains the lifeline of [modern Egypt](/article/modern-egypt) and the heart of its [tourism](/article/egypt-tourism), even as its waters are increasingly contested among the nations that share its vast basin.
"Egypt," wrote Herodotus, "is the gift of the river." Twenty-five centuries later, it still is.

title: The Pharaoh
lede: Neither wholly human nor wholly divine, the pharaoh stood at the hinge of the Egyptian universe — guarantor of cosmic order, head of a vast bureaucratic state, and the living image of a god on Earth.

maps.title: Seats of royal power
maps.caption: Memphis, Thebes and Pi-Ramesses — capitals from which pharaohs ruled the two lands.

images.title: The double crown
images.caption: The Pschent — the White Crown of Upper Egypt nested in the Red Crown of Lower Egypt.

images.title: Ramesses II at Abu Simbel
images.caption: Four colossal seated statues of the New Kingdom's most prolific builder-king.

videos.title: Coronation of a god-king
videos.caption: The rites that transformed a man into the living Horus and guarantor of Maat.

citations.label: The British Museum — kingship
citations.source: The British Museum, Department of Egypt and Sudan

body.text: Horus on the Throne

The word *pharaoh* comes from **per-aa**, "the great house" — originally the palace itself, later its royal occupant. The king was the indispensable link between humanity and the gods. While he lived he was the earthly embodiment of **Horus**; when he died he became **Osiris**, and his successor took up the falcon's role. Kingship was therefore not just political office but a cosmic function: through the pharaoh, maat was maintained and the very order of the world renewed.
Crowns, Names, and Titles
Royal identity was written in regalia and language:

items: The **double crown** (*Pschent*) united the White Crown of Upper Egypt and the Red Crown of Lower Egypt — a wearable claim to rule both lands.
The **crook and flail** signalled the king as shepherd and provider.
The **false beard** and **uraeus** (the rearing cobra) marked divine authority.
Each king bore a **fivefold titulary** of throne-names and birth-names, framed in protective oval **cartouches**.

body.title: Pharaohs were not all men
body.text: The throne was overwhelmingly male, but several women ruled in their own right — most notably **Hatshepsut**, one of Egypt's most successful and prolific builders, who had herself depicted with the traditional regalia of kingship.
The Machinery of Rule

A god-king still needed a government. Beneath the pharaoh stood the **vizier**, the chief administrator, and an army of literate **scribes** who ran the granaries, the tax rolls, the courts, and the great building projects. Because wealth was measured in grain and stored centrally, the state reached into every village. This made Egypt one of history's first highly organised bureaucratic states — and explains how it could marshal the labour to raise the [Pyramids of Giza](/article/pyramids-of-giza).
The greatest builders — Khufu, Hatshepsut, Ramesses II — measured their reigns in stone, leaving monuments designed to proclaim a single, eternal message: *the king endures.*
That ambition to endure echoes across millennia into the symbols of [modern Egypt](/article/modern-egypt), where the pharaohs remain a source of national identity and pride.

title: The Planets
lede: From scorched Mercury to frigid Neptune, the eight planets of the Solar System are a gallery of wildly different worlds — rocky and gaseous, cratered and storm-wracked, ringed and barren. Together they reveal how varied a single star's family of planets can be.

maps.title: The eight planets to scale
maps.caption: The four small terrestrial worlds and the four giants, in order of distance from the Sun.

images.title: Earth, the blue marble
images.caption: The only known world with liquid water on its surface and life — so far, the one planet we know to be inhabited.

images.title: Jupiter and the Great Red Spot
images.caption: The largest planet, a banded gas giant whose centuries-old storm is wider than the Earth.

images.title: Saturn's rings
images.caption: A dazzling system of countless icy particles, only a few tens of metres thick yet tens of thousands of kilometres wide.

videos.title: A tour of the eight planets
videos.caption: A flythrough from Mercury to Neptune, comparing size, climate, and character.

videos.title: How we weigh and measure a planet
videos.caption: Orbits, transits, and the tricks astronomers use to map worlds they cannot visit.

body.text: What Makes a Planet

A planet, as the International Astronomical Union defines it, is a body that orbits the Sun, is massive enough for its own gravity to pull it into a round shape, and has cleared its orbital path of other debris. By that measure the Solar System has exactly **eight** planets. They fall into two clear families, a divide inherited from how the [Solar System](/article/the-solar-system) formed.

The four **terrestrial planets** — Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars — are small, dense, and rocky, with solid surfaces you could stand on. The four **giant planets** — Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune — are vast balls of gas and ice with no real surface at all, surrounded by rings and swarms of moons.

body.title: The order, and a way to remember it
body.text: Outward from the Sun: **Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune**. A classic mnemonic is "My Very Educated Mother Just Served Us Nachos." Note that the largest planets sit farthest out — a consequence of ice being abundant only in the cold outer Solar System.
The Rocky Inner Worlds

**Mercury**, closest to the Sun, is the smallest planet — a cratered, airless ball not much larger than the Moon. With almost no atmosphere to hold heat, it swings from scorching day to freezing night more violently than any other planet.

**Venus** is Earth's near-twin in size, but a hellish one. A thick atmosphere of carbon dioxide traps heat in a runaway **greenhouse effect**, making its surface hotter than Mercury's — around 465°C, hot enough to melt lead — beneath clouds of sulphuric acid. It is a sobering example of how a climate can go catastrophically wrong.

**Earth** is, so far, unique: the only known world with liquid water on its surface, a protective magnetic field, a breathable atmosphere, and life. Its single large [Moon](/article/the-moon) stabilises its tilt and drives the tides.

**Mars**, the rusty red desert world, is the most Earth-like of our neighbours and the great focus of exploration — a planet that once had rivers and lakes, and the most likely place to search for past life. Its story is told in full in [Mars](/article/mars).
The Giants of the Outer System
Beyond the asteroid belt, the planets become enormous.

items: **Jupiter** is the king — more than twice as massive as all the other planets combined. This banded gas giant is wrapped in storms, the greatest of which, the **Great Red Spot**, has raged for centuries and is wider than the Earth. Its powerful gravity helps shield the inner planets from stray comets.
**Saturn** is famous for its breathtaking **rings**, a glittering system of countless ice and rock particles. For all its size, Saturn is so light for its volume that it would float in water.
**Uranus** is an ice giant tipped onto its side, rolling around the Sun at an extreme tilt that gives it decades-long seasons. Its pale blue-green colour comes from methane in its atmosphere.
**Neptune**, the most distant planet, is a deep blue world of supersonic winds — the fastest in the Solar System — so far from the Sun that one of its years lasts 165 of ours.

body.text: Worlds Within Worlds

The giant planets are each surrounded by their own miniature systems of moons, some of which are among the most intriguing places in the Solar System. Jupiter's **Europa** and Saturn's **Enceladus** appear to hide liquid-water oceans beneath icy crusts; Saturn's **Titan**, larger than the planet Mercury, has a dense atmosphere and seas of liquid methane. If life exists elsewhere in the Solar System, these ocean moons are among the leading places to look.

body.title: Where did Pluto go?
body.text: For 76 years Pluto was counted as the ninth planet. In 2006 it was reclassified as a **dwarf planet**, because it has not cleared its orbit — it is one of many icy bodies in the Kuiper Belt. The change reflected a deeper discovery: that the Solar System holds whole populations of small worlds Pluto belongs to.
Planets Beyond the Sun

For most of history, the eight planets were the only ones we knew. That changed in **1995**, when astronomers detected the first planet orbiting another Sun-like star — a discovery later honoured with a Nobel Prize. These worlds around other stars are called **exoplanets**, and we have now confirmed thousands of them, with billions more thought to exist across the Milky Way.

The variety is staggering: scorching "hot Jupiters" that hug their stars, vast "super-Earths" with no analogue at home, and rocky worlds orbiting in the **habitable zone**, where temperatures might allow liquid water. Each discovery widens the question that the planets first raise: how common are worlds like Earth, and might any of them be home to life?
A Gallery of Possibilities

Taken together, the planets are a natural laboratory. Venus shows what a runaway greenhouse can do; Mars shows a world that lost its water and air; Jupiter shows how a giant can shepherd and protect its neighbours; the ocean moons hint that habitable conditions may be far more widespread than once believed.

Studying our own eight worlds is what lets us read the billions being found elsewhere — and it shapes our ambitions for the journey ahead, from robotic explorers to human footprints on other worlds, the subject of [space missions](/article/space-missions) and [the future of space](/article/the-future-of-space).

toc.text: The Giants of the Outer System

title: The Pyramids of Giza
lede: Three colossal tombs raised on a desert plateau over forty-five centuries ago — the most precise, most enduring, and only surviving wonder of the ancient world, and a monument to the organisational genius of the Old Kingdom.

maps.title: The Giza plateau
maps.caption: The pyramids of Khufu, Khafre and Menkaure with the Great Sphinx, southwest of modern Cairo.

images.title: The Great Pyramid of Khufu
images.caption: Originally 146.6 m tall and clad in polished white limestone that would have blazed in the sun.

images.title: The Great Sphinx
images.caption: A limestone lion with a human face, almost certainly bearing the likeness of Khafre.

images.title: Interior — the Grand Gallery
images.caption: A soaring corbel-vaulted passage rising to the King's Chamber inside Khufu's pyramid.

videos.title: Building the Great Pyramid
videos.caption: How ramps, levers, and a vast organised workforce raised 2.3 million blocks.

body.text: A Wonder That Outlasted the Rest

Of the **Seven Wonders of the Ancient World**, only one still stands: the **Great Pyramid of Khufu** at Giza. Completed around 2560 BCE, it held the record as the tallest structure made by human hands for nearly **3,800 years**, until the cathedral spires of medieval Europe finally surpassed it. Beside it rise the pyramids of **Khafre** (which looks taller because it sits on higher ground and still keeps a cap of its original casing) and the smaller **Menkaure**, guarded by the **Great Sphinx**.
Precision Without Iron

What stuns engineers is not just the size but the **accuracy**. The Great Pyramid's square base, some 230 metres on a side, is level to within a couple of centimetres, and its faces are aligned to true north with an error of only a fraction of a degree — achieved without iron tools, the compass, or modern surveying instruments.

items: An estimated **2.3 million blocks**, averaging over two tonnes each.
Original height **146.6 m**; a smooth casing of fine white limestone, now largely stripped away.
Internal chambers, relieving stones, and the soaring **Grand Gallery**, all cut and fitted with extraordinary care.

body.title: Not built by slaves
body.text: The old image of whip-driven slaves is a myth. Archaeology at the workers' village shows a paid, fed, and housed workforce of skilled craftsmen and rotating labourers — organised, provisioned, and even given medical care by the Egyptian state.
Tombs Aligned to the Sky

The pyramids were **royal tombs**, designed to launch the dead king into the heavens and eternal life. Their orientation was no accident: the Egyptians used the stars to fix true north, tying their architecture to the rhythms of the sky — a connection explored in [Egyptian astronomy](/article/egyptian-astronomy). Causeways linked each pyramid to a valley temple by the Nile, framing the whole complex as a machine for resurrection.
The shape itself may evoke the **benben** — the primeval mound that rose from the waters of creation, and the petrified rays of the sun god descending to the earth.

The pyramids are the supreme statement of a wider tradition of sacred building that runs through Egyptian temples and on into the story of [ancient architecture](/article/ancient-architecture). Today they remain the single most visited monument in the country — the gateway to [Egyptian tourism](/article/egypt-tourism).

toc.text: A Wonder That Outlasted the Rest

title: The Solar System
lede: One ordinary star, eight planets, scores of moons, and countless lumps of rock and ice — all bound together by gravity and born from a single collapsing cloud 4.6 billion years ago. The Solar System is the small, well-lit neighbourhood that humanity calls home.

maps.title: The layout of the Solar System
maps.caption: The Sun, the four rocky inner planets, the asteroid belt, the four giant outer planets, and the icy realms beyond.

maps.title: The Sun's reach
maps.caption: The heliosphere — the vast bubble of solar wind that marks the boundary between the Sun's domain and interstellar space.

images.title: The Sun, our anchor
images.caption: Holding 99.86 percent of all the mass in the Solar System, the Sun's gravity governs everything that orbits it.

images.title: The family portrait
images.caption: A pale blue dot — Earth seen by Voyager 1 from beyond Neptune, a mote of dust in a sunbeam.

images.title: The protoplanetary disk
images.caption: A young star ringed by the dusty disk in which planets coalesce, much as ours did 4.6 billion years ago.

videos.title: How the Solar System formed
videos.caption: From a spinning cloud of gas and dust to a star with a family of worlds.

videos.title: A tour from the Sun to the Oort Cloud
videos.caption: Crossing the true scale of the Solar System, from the inner planets to its distant icy edge.

citations.label: NASA — How the Solar System formed

body.text: Born from a Cloud

About 4.6 billion years ago, in an unremarkable part of the Milky Way, a giant cloud of gas and dust — perhaps nudged by the shockwave of a nearby [supernova](/article/stars) — began to collapse under its own gravity. As it fell inward it spun faster and flattened into a swirling disk, with most of the material piling up at the centre.

When that central mass grew hot and dense enough, fusion ignited and the **Sun** switched on. The leftover material in the surrounding disk clumped together grain by grain, building first into pebbles, then boulders, then worlds. Everything in the Solar System — every planet, moon, asteroid, and comet — condensed from that same primordial disk, which is why they all orbit in roughly the same plane and the same direction to this day.

body.title: One star, almost all the mass
body.text: The Sun contains about 99.86 percent of all the mass in the Solar System. Everything else — all eight planets, every moon, and every asteroid combined — makes up barely a tenth of one percent. The Solar System is, overwhelmingly, the Sun plus some rounding error.
A System in Two Halves

The Solar System divides neatly into an inner and an outer region, separated by the asteroid belt. The split is a direct legacy of how it formed: close to the young Sun it was too hot for ices to survive, so only rock and metal could condense, while farther out, beyond the **frost line**, ice was abundant and worlds could grow huge.

items: **The inner Solar System** holds the four small, rocky **terrestrial planets** — Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars — dense balls of rock and iron.
**The asteroid belt** lies between Mars and Jupiter: a band of rocky debris, the rubble of a planet that never formed, kept stirred up by Jupiter's gravity.

**The outer Solar System** is the realm of the **giant planets** — Jupiter and Saturn, the gas giants, and Uranus and Neptune, the ice giants — each far larger than Earth and wreathed in moons and rings.

body.text: These worlds, and what makes each one distinct, are the subject of [the planets](/article/the-planets).
The Sun: Engine and Anchor

The **Sun** is the heart of it all. Its gravity holds every planet in its orbit, and its light and warmth drive the weather, the seasons, and almost all life on Earth. A stream of charged particles called the **solar wind** blows constantly outward, carving a vast protective bubble — the **heliosphere** — around the entire planetary system.

The Sun is a middling [star](/article/stars), roughly halfway through a ten-billion-year life. For now it is remarkably steady, but it is not eternal. In several billion years it will swell into a red giant, scorching the inner planets and reshaping the Solar System for its final act — a long-term fate traced in [the future of space](/article/the-future-of-space).
Moons, Rings, and Rubble
Planets are only the beginning. The Solar System teems with smaller bodies, many of them worlds in their own right.

More than **two hundred moons** orbit the planets, and some are astonishing places: Jupiter's **Europa** and Saturn's **Enceladus** hide oceans of liquid water beneath shells of ice; Saturn's **Titan** has rivers and lakes of liquid methane under a thick orange haze; and Earth's own [Moon](/article/the-moon) shaped the conditions for life on our planet.

Then there are the wanderers. **Asteroids** are rocky leftovers, concentrated in the belt but scattered widely. **Comets** are icy bodies that grow glowing tails when the Sun's heat boils off their surface, swinging in from the cold outer reaches on long, looping orbits.

body.title: Why Pluto was reclassified
body.text: In 2006 the International Astronomical Union defined a "planet" as a body that, among other things, has cleared its orbital neighbourhood of other objects. Pluto shares its region with many similar icy bodies, so it was reclassified as a **dwarf planet** — a category it shares with Ceres, Eris, and others. It was not a demotion so much as a recognition that Pluto belongs to a whole new population of worlds.
The Distant Edges

Far beyond Neptune lies the **Kuiper Belt**, a broad ring of icy bodies — Pluto among them — that are frozen relics of the Solar System's birth. Farther still, perhaps a thousand times more distant, astronomers believe a vast spherical shell of icy debris called the **Oort Cloud** surrounds the whole system, marking the true outer boundary of the Sun's gravitational reach. It is so remote that a comet from there can take millions of years to complete a single orbit.

The scale is hard to grasp. If the Sun were the size of a beach ball, Earth would be a peppercorn some 25 metres away, and the edge of the Oort Cloud would lie kilometres distant. The Solar System is mostly empty space.
Reaching Beyond

Only one human-made object has crossed the heliosphere into interstellar space: **Voyager 1**, launched in 1977, which slipped past the Sun's bubble in 2012 and now drifts among the stars, still faintly calling home. Its journey is the opening chapter of humanity's reach beyond our own neighbourhood — a story continued in [space missions](/article/space-missions).

For all our exploration, the Solar System remains the only place we have ever directly visited, the only star we have studied up close, and — so far — the only place known to harbour life. Understanding it is how we calibrate everything else: a single, well-mapped example against which we measure the billions of other planetary systems now being discovered across the galaxy.
